templates.name: Slack Notifier
templates.description: Post a message to Slack using your own credential from the Vault — the 'bring your own API' on-ramp.
templates.whatItDoes: Takes a message (and optional channel), reads your Slack credential from the Vault at runtime, and posts to Slack via a direct `fetch` — either `chat.postMessage` with a bot token or an incoming-webhook URL. The teaching template for connecting an external service: store a secret, read it at runtime, call an API. No secret is ever baked into code, and a dryRun / no-credential guard lets `run_local` trace the whole graph offline. Slack is just the hook — repoint the endpoint + Vault key to call any API.

steps.name: validate
steps.description: Check the message and resolve config (auth mode, channel).

steps.name: post
steps.description: Read the Slack credential from the Vault and post the message.

steps.name: posted
steps.description: Return the post confirmation (channel + timestamp).

steps.name: failed
steps.description: The Slack API call errored.

steps.name: rejected
steps.description: Input failed validation; nothing was posted.

templates.name: Web Research Digest
templates.description: Search the web for a topic and return a concise, sourced digest.
templates.whatItDoes: Takes a topic, runs a web search through the Sapiom search capability, then condenses the top results into a short digest with source links. A legible "it did a thing" flagship — one metered capability, an obvious output.

steps.name: search
steps.description: Query the web for the topic.

steps.name: summarize
steps.description: Condense the results into a sourced digest.

templates.name: Hello Agent
templates.description: The minimal single-step agent — a smoke test for the build → deploy → run path.
templates.whatItDoes: Validates an input name and returns a greeting. The smallest valid definition — use it to confirm your MCP install and deploy path work end to end before reaching for a capability.

steps.name: greet
steps.description: Validate the input and return a greeting.

templates.name: Wait-for-Webhook
templates.description: Durable pause/resume — start a slow external job, suspend at $0 until a webhook callback fires, then decide.
templates.whatItDoes: Starts a slow external async job and registers a resume contract, then suspends the run indefinitely at $0 via pauseUntilSignal — no polling, no held worker, no billed idle time. When an external webhook/callback fires the signal, the run resumes exactly where it left off: the callback payload becomes the resumed step's input, an LLM summarizes it, and the run branches to accept or reject. The sharpest showcase of the platform's durability differentiator.

steps.name: kickoff
steps.description: Start the external async job, register the resume contract, then pause until the callback signal (resumes at 'decide').

steps.name: decide
steps.description: Resume target — its input is the callback payload; summarize it with an LLM and branch.

steps.name: accept
steps.description: Terminal branch: the callback result was accepted.

steps.name: reject
steps.description: Terminal branch: the callback result was rejected.

templates.name: Scheduled Research Brief
templates.description: On a cron cadence, research a topic and email a short, LLM-curated, sourced brief.
templates.whatItDoes: The scheduled, LLM-curated, delivered evolution of web-research-digest. On each tick it web-searches a topic, scrapes the top candidates for full text, hands them to an LLM (models.run — the live x402-served model) to rank and curate a short sourced brief, then delivers it by email. Ships with a schedule input (topic + cron cadence) so it reads as a standing "morning brief" agent; a dryRun guard gates the real send. Fork web-research-digest for a one-shot digest, this for a standing brief.

steps.name: search
steps.description: Web-search the topic for candidate sources.

steps.name: scrape
steps.description: Read the top candidates for full article text.

steps.name: curate
steps.description: Rank and curate the findings into a sourced brief with an LLM.

steps.name: deliver
steps.description: Email the brief to the configured recipient (dry-run skips the send).

templates.name: Scene → Images → Video
templates.description: Turn a scene description into a stitched short video: LLM shot list → per-shot keyframes → async image-to-video → stitch.
templates.whatItDoes: The richest onboarding template — a real multi-step generative pipeline. An LLM decomposes one scene into a style/identity bible plus an ordered shot list; each shot becomes a keyframe image (fan-out); each keyframe is animated into a short clip via an async image-to-video job that pauses the workflow until a FAL webhook resumes it; the clips are stitched into one video. Exercises three metered capabilities together and shows off async pause/resume + fan-out. A dryRun input returns the plan only, skipping the (higher-cost) image + video generation. Pricier than the other templates — the estimated per-run cost card will be visibly higher.

steps.name: decompose
steps.description: LLM turns the scene into a style/identity bible + an ordered shot list. Terminates here with the plan when dryRun is set.

steps.name: keyframes
steps.description: Generate one keyframe image per shot (fan-out), each persisted for a durable fileId.

steps.name: animate
steps.description: Launch an async image-to-video job for one shot and pause until the FAL webhook resumes.

steps.name: collect
steps.description: Record the finished clip, then loop back to animate the next shot or advance to stitch.

steps.name: stitch
steps.description: Concat the clips into one video (FAL ffmpeg merge), async — pause until ready.

steps.name: finalize
steps.description: Return the stitched video's durable videoFileId + downloadUrl.

templates.name: Preview Keep-Alive
templates.description: A durable cron heartbeat that relaunches a dead sandbox preview — self-healing, no human redeploy.
templates.whatItDoes: On each scheduled run, probes a preview's health path. If healthy, it's a terminal no-op — so it never stacks a second process onto a live one. If down, it re-attaches the target sandbox and calls deployPreview (source fs) to rebuild + restart the uploaded code at the same stable URL, with env assembled from a Postgres handle and vault secrets read at runtime. One deployed definition keeps N previews alive — one schedule each.

steps.name: check
steps.description: Probe the preview's health path; route to heal only when it's down.

steps.name: healthy
steps.description: App is serving — terminal no-op.

steps.name: heal
steps.description: Re-attach the sandbox and redeploy the fs source; assemble env from database.get + vault.get.

steps.name: healed
steps.description: Relaunch succeeded — report url/status.

steps.name: heal_failed
steps.description: Relaunch failed — surface the deployPreview logs.

templates.name: Human-in-the-Loop Approval
templates.description: Do reversible work, pause for a human approval signal before committing, then commit via a ranked sequential-fallback loop.
templates.whatItDoes: The "ask before you commit/spend" pattern. The agent does reversible prep — parse the request (models.run), rank the candidates by fit, and notify the approver (email) — then blocks on a durable pauseUntilSignal so the run survives the wait at $0. A reject reverts to a safe state and terminates. An approve enters a ranked sequential-fallback loop: a provisional (non-committing) offer to the top candidate, a pause for their confirm, and on decline/timeout it advances to the next candidate. The single irreversible/expensive action lands in commit only after a human approved AND a candidate accepted; exhausting the ranked list escalates to a human instead of silently failing.

steps.name: parse
steps.description: Parse the free-text request into structured intent with an LLM.

steps.name: rank
steps.description: Rank the candidates by fit (not just cost) with an LLM.

steps.name: notifyApprover
steps.description: Email the approver the ranked recommendation, then pause until the approval signal (resumes at 'onDecision').

steps.name: onDecision
steps.description: Resume target — its input is the approval payload; approve enters the offer loop, reject reverts, no candidates escalates.

steps.name: revert
steps.description: Terminal branch: rejected — revert to a safe state, nothing committed.

steps.name: offer
steps.description: Provisional (non-committing) offer to the top-ranked candidate, then pause until their confirm (resumes at 'resolve').

steps.name: resolve
steps.description: Resume target — its input is the confirm payload; accept commits, decline/timeout advances to the next candidate or escalates when exhausted.

steps.name: commit
steps.description: The single irreversible/expensive action — reached only after approval AND a candidate accept; a dryRun guard makes it a no-op offline.

steps.name: escalate
steps.description: Terminal branch: ranked list exhausted (or no candidates) — escalate to a human channel.

templates.name: Eval Gate
templates.description: An LLM-judge quality gate — score an output against your rubric, then branch publish/revise on a threshold.
templates.whatItDoes: Renders a judge prompt from YOUR rubric, calls an LLM via ctx.sapiom.models.run, and parses a [0,1] score. A pure gate step branches publish vs revise on score >= threshold; the terminal steps return { decision, score, threshold, rationale } — you decide what publish/revise mean. We own the harness (default judge prompt + score parser); the rubric is yours, so it doubles as an eval-authoring on-ramp. Because it's a deployable agent, a parent workflow can launch it as a child to self-grade its own output and branch on the returned decision.

steps.name: judge
steps.description: Build the judge prompt from your rubric, call the LLM (models.run), parse the score.

steps.name: gate
steps.description: Pure branch: score >= threshold ⇒ publish, else revise. No capability call.

steps.name: publish
steps.description: Terminal: output met the threshold. Return { decision: 'publish', score, … }.

steps.name: revise
steps.description: Terminal: output below the threshold. Return { decision: 'revise', score, … }.

templates.name: The Brain
templates.description: A fleet orchestrator meta-workflow — a brain that scans its fleet, chooses a play from a fixed allow-list, and launches the right child workflow.
templates.whatItDoes: The flagship "agents managing agents" template: a meta-workflow that coordinates a fleet of child workflows through a scan → assess → actuate → report loop. Deterministic code senses (folds a Postgres-backed event bus into crisp situations like no_child_ran_today / cooldown_due / stale_no_result) and acts (launches children behind six guardrails with per-day idempotency); a constrained LLM only chooses a "play" from a fixed allow-list, re-validated in code. A brain is not another limb — it reasons about the whole picture and launches the right member, never doing irreversible work itself. Ships with dryRun (trace the loop offline) and observeOnly (report what it would launch without launching).

steps.name: scan
steps.description: Read the event bus, fold the log into cadence facts, compute deterministic situations.

steps.name: assess
steps.description: Hand the situations to a constrained LLM that may only pick a play from a fixed allow-list; re-validate the JSON.

steps.name: actuate
steps.description: Execute the plan behind six guardrails; launch each due member as a child workflow and append a member.launched row.

steps.name: report
steps.description: Post a briefing to a low-noise channel, append a brain.briefing row, advance the cursor, terminate.
